perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time by giving thirty (30) days prior written notification.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either Party at any time by giving ten (10) days prior written notification.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 30 days to 10 days may violate state laws requiring longer notice periods for contract termination, especially where significant business relationships exist.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Franchise Practices Act (N.J. Stat. Ann. § 56:10-5)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text shortens the notice period for contract termination to 10 days, potentially conflicting with the New Jersey Franchise Practices Act, which requires a longer notice period (60-90 days) for terminating franchise agreements, depending on the reason for termination. While this is an NDA and not a franchise agreement, the general principle of reasonable notice applies to contractual relationships, and 10 days could be deemed unreasonably short in certain commercial contexts under New Jersey law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract provides a 30-day notice for termination, while the changed contract reduces it to 10 days. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts the principle of reasonable notice, particularly in commercial contexts under New Jersey law, where longer notice periods (60-90 days) may be required for terminating franchise agreements. Although the snippets are not useful, the Law Explanation suggests that shortening the notice period to 10 days could be deemed unreasonably short in certain commercial contexts, thus creating a legal contradiction based on the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the 10-day period violates or undermines the legal obligation for reasonable notice.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations under this agreement shall continue to remain in force for a period of five (5) years from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations under this agreement shall continue to remain in force perpetually from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying that the obligations remain in force perpetually could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, conflicting with general principles of contract law, especially in states like California where such restraints are disfavored.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text enforces the agreement perpetually, which acts as a restraint on trade and profession, violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind are void, unless subject to a specific exception (e.g., sale of a business).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a fixed term of five years with 'perpetually', which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation identify as a potential violation of California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. By creating a perpetual obligation, the changed text could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus contradicting the law. The change is not a minor adjustment but a fundamental alteration that introduces a perpetual restriction, making the contradiction explicit and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, and the relationship between (Your Company Name) and Petro- Lubricant Testing Laboratories, Inc. under this Agreement, shall in all respects be interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of the United States of America and the State of (Your State), and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of those courts in relation to any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (whether based in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, and the relationship between (Your Company Name) and Petro- Lubricant Testing Laboratories, Inc. under this Agreement, shall in all respects be interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of courts in Russia in relation to any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (whether based in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to a foreign country (Russia) might violate US export control laws or sanctions if the agreement involves technology or information subject to these restrictions, and would make enforcing the agreement against a US company difficult if Russian courts are involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Choosing Russian courts could potentially hinder the enforcement of U.S. export control laws (EAR). If the technology or information exchanged under the NDA is subject to the EAR, resolving disputes in Russia might obstruct U.S. authorities from ensuring compliance with export restrictions, especially if Russia has different views on these restrictions, because EAR requires US jurisdiction.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.

perturbation.justification: The changed text designates Russian courts as the exclusive jurisdiction for dispute resolution and changes the governing law to that of Delaware. This alteration contradicts U.S. export control laws, specifically the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), as it could impede U.S. authorities' ability to enforce these regulations if disputes are resolved in Russia, especially if the agreement involves technology or information subject to EAR. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight this concern, noting that Russian courts might have different views on these restrictions, potentially obstructing U.S. compliance efforts. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and Commerce Control List (CCL), indicating the U.S. government's authority to regulate exports. By potentially moving jurisdiction to Russia, the agreement risks undermining U.S. regulatory oversight.

perturbation.original_text: At the conclusion of the Evaluation Petro-Lube shall either return promptly to (Your Company Name) or it's Assigns, or at (Your Company Name)'s option, destroy any remaining Sample in whatever form then in its possession or control and verify such to (Your Company Name).
perturbation.changed_text: At the conclusion of the Evaluation, Petro-Lube may, at its sole discretion, retain any remaining Sample in whatever form then in its possession or control for internal research purposes, and there is no obligation to notify or obtain consent from (Your Company Name).
perturbation.explanation: This change allows Petro-Lube to retain samples without consent, which may violate state laws regarding trade secrets or confidential information, especially if the samples contain proprietary formulas or processes. If the sample contains trade secrets, unauthorized retention might constitute misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) - adopted in most US states, e.g., New Jersey Trade Secrets Act (N.J.S.A. 56:15-1)
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing Petro-Lube to retain samples without consent or notification, the modified text potentially violates the UTSA (as adopted in New Jersey, N.J.S.A. 56:15-1 et seq.). If the samples contain trade secrets, unauthorized retention and use for internal research could be considered misappropriation of trade secrets, which is illegal under the UTSA because the definition of misappropriation covers 'acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means'.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows Petro-Lube to retain samples for internal research without consent, potentially misappropriating trade secrets if the samples contain proprietary information. The Law Explanation supports this by referencing the UTSA and the definition of misappropriation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 does not contain legal content directly, the Law Explanation's invocation of the UTSA, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, demonstrates a plausible contradiction if the samples contain trade secrets, because the original text required return or destruction of the sample, and now the changed text allows for indefinite retention, violating the UTSA if the sample is a trade secret.
